comments:
This paper reports a useful and well-engineered system extension (STEX+) that advances an existing LaTeX-based semantic-authoring stack (STEX → LaTeXML → OMDoc → XHTML+MathML) to support flexible, user-definable RDFa metadata vocabularies and Linked Data publishing. The practical SAMSDocs case study from software engineering motivates the work well: the authors identify the multi-dimensional nature of the required annotations (mathematical, project/process, organizational) and show how STEX+ (notably the \keydef mechanism) enables co-development of documents and lightweight ontologies while preserving traditional PDF workflows. The integration with existing components (JOBAD, JOMDoc, TNTBase, Virtuoso/SPARQL) and the discussion of interactive services and querying demonstrate a functioning toolchain rather than just a concept.

Strengths: clear problem motivation (the “authoring problem”), an invasive approach that preserves authoring habits, concrete implementation details, and an end-to-end deployment with examples of services. The paper situates STEX+ in relation to other efforts (SALT, SOBOLEO, loomp) and argues for the benefits of combining vocabulary definition and document annotation in the same macro-based frontend.

Weaknesses / areas for improvement: the evaluation is largely anecdotal and implementation-oriented — there is no user study, quantitative performance or scalability evaluation, nor a systematic comparison of developer/author productivity against alternatives. The related-work discussion, while present, is brief and could more deeply contrast strengths/limitations of STEX+ relative to SALT and GUI-based editors (e.g., usability tradeoffs, learning curve). The paper would benefit from a clearer statement of limitations (e.g., maintenance costs for vocabularies, namespace/conflict management, how to handle large distributed Linked Data scenarios) and from inclusion of concrete examples of vocabulary reuse or alignment (owl:sameAs practices) in the wild.

Suggestions: add (even small) user feedback or case-study metrics (time to formalize typical document fragments, number of macros/vocab terms produced), describe performance characteristics of server-side triple handling for larger collections, and expand on guidelines for migrating legacy LaTeX collections and for collaboration on vocabularies. A short how-to on producing RDF/XML or embedding XMP for PDF consumers would increase practical value.

Overall, the paper makes a solid, practical contribution to semantic authoring and MKM toolchains. With modest additions on empirical evaluation and a deeper discussion of limitations and adoption issues, it would be an even stronger paper. I recommend acceptance as a system paper.
